title: Paralegals and legal assistants
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital and document-based: legal research, drafting, organizing files, reviewing transcripts, and coordinating case materials are all core tasks where AI is rapidly improving. Some in-person trial support and client interaction remain, but AI can substantially automate or accelerate much of the occupation’s main work, likely reducing labor demand per case.

title: Medical transcriptionists
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, consisting of converting, editing, and entering dictated medical information into standardized records on a computer. AI speech recognition and natural language processing already perform much of the core work today, with humans increasingly limited to review and error correction, which is why the occupation is already declining.

title: Tour and travel guides
rationale: The core of this job is physical, place-based, and highly interpersonal: escorting groups, managing logistics in real time, and engaging visitors on-site. AI can help with peripheral tasks like research, itinerary planning, translation, marketing, and recordkeeping, but it is unlikely to replace the in-person presence, adaptability, and customer interaction at the center of the occupation.

title: Calibration technologists and technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on: inspecting, adjusting, testing, and maintaining physical measurement devices in labs, plants, and other on-site settings. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, diagnostics, and analyzing test data, but it does not remove the need for physical manipulation, precision adjustment, and real-world verification against standards.

title: Waiters and waitresses
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and in-person: greeting diners, taking orders, carrying food, checking on tables, and handling the live restaurant experience. AI can affect peripheral and adjacent tasks through kiosks, digital ordering, and payment automation, but it does not replace most of the hands-on service and real-time customer interaction.

title: Athletes and sports competitors
rationale: The core work of athletes is intensely physical, in-person performance that depends on bodily skill, training, and real-time competition, which AI cannot replace directly. AI may help with peripheral tasks like video analysis, strategy, scouting opponents, and training optimization, but it does not substitute for the athlete’s on-field or on-court role.

title: Labor relations specialists
rationale: This job combines substantial digital knowledge work—drafting policies, reviewing agreements, analyzing compliance, preparing materials, and documenting cases—with high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and dispute resolution. AI can meaningfully automate research, writing, summarization, and case preparation, but in-person relationship management, trust-building, and judgment in tense labor-management situations keep exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Flight attendants
rationale: Flight attendants’ core work is physical and in-person: ensuring passenger safety, responding to emergencies, enforcing regulations, and providing onboard service in a live aircraft environment. AI may automate peripheral tasks such as reporting, scheduling, or some customer communication, but it cannot replace the legal, physical, and interpersonal presence required onboard.

title: Butchers
rationale: Butchers’ core work is highly physical and hands-on: cutting, deboning, packaging meat, handling tools, maintaining sanitation, and serving customers in person. AI may help only at the margins with inventory tracking, ordering, and sales analysis, but it does little to automate the central manual and on-site tasks.

title: Community health workers
rationale: Community health workers spend much of their time in the field doing outreach, home visits, coaching, advocacy, and culturally sensitive interpersonal support—core tasks that require human trust and physical presence. AI can help with documentation, translation, resource matching, and case-management logistics, but it is unlikely to replace the relationship-based, community-embedded nature of the work.

title: Retail sales workers
rationale: Retail sales work depends heavily on physical presence in stores, face-to-face customer service, stocking, and handling merchandise, which limits direct automation by AI. However, AI and related technologies can reduce labor needs indirectly through better online shopping, automated checkout, inventory systems, and product recommendation tools, so the occupation faces meaningful but not extreme exposure.

title: Survey researchers
rationale: Survey researchers do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing questionnaires, analyzing data with statistical software, troubleshooting sampling issues, and presenting findings. AI can already assist substantially with survey drafting, data analysis, visualization, and reporting, and newer data-mining tools may reduce demand for traditional survey work, though human judgment still matters for methodology, validity, and interpretation.

title: Concierges
rationale: Concierge work has meaningful AI exposure because many core tasks—answering routine questions, making reservations, handling messages, and coordinating services—can be assisted or partly automated by apps and AI agents. But the job still depends heavily on physical presence, real-time customer service, trust, and handling in-person requests in hotels, residences, and offices, which limits full automation.

title: Postsecondary education administrators
rationale: This is largely knowledge and administrative work—reviewing applications, analyzing student data, managing records, preparing communications, scheduling, and handling routine advising—all areas where AI can significantly assist or partially automate tasks. But the role also depends on human judgment, institutional accountability, sensitive student and faculty interactions, and on-campus coordination, which limits full replacement.

title: Bartenders
rationale: Bartending is centered on physical, in-person service: mixing and serving drinks, checking IDs, monitoring intoxication, handling payments, cleaning, and managing a live social environment. title: Financial examiners
rationale: Financial examiners do predominantly digital knowledge work: reviewing financial statements and loan files, analyzing regulatory impacts, assessing risk, and writing compliance reports. AI can increasingly automate document review, anomaly detection, risk analysis, and report drafting, though human judgment, accountability, and some onsite examination of institutions keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Insurance underwriters
rationale: Insurance underwriting is highly exposed because the core work is digital information processing: analyzing applications, assessing risk, and setting coverage and premiums using software. The BLS description already notes that automated underwriting tools handle many routine cases and are expected to reduce employment further, leaving mainly more complex judgment-heavy cases for humans.

title: Models
rationale: Modeling depends heavily on physical appearance, in-person photo shoots, runway shows, and live brand representation, which limits full automation. But AI-generated imagery and image reuse can directly reduce demand for human models in digital advertising and other visual media, creating meaningful indirect exposure.

title: Gambling services workers
rationale: Most gambling services work is in-person and physical: dealing games, taking bets, monitoring customers, paying winnings, and supervising casino floors all require real-time human presence and customer interaction. AI may reduce headcount indirectly through online gambling, automated betting, surveillance, and administrative tools, but it does not readily replace the core on-floor service work in physical casinos.

title: Natural sciences managers
rationale: Natural sciences managers do substantial digital knowledge work—planning, budgeting, reviewing research, reporting, and communicating—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in many core tasks. But the occupation also depends on leadership, scientific judgment, cross-functional coordination, hiring, and oversight of labs or fieldwork, which keeps exposure below the very highest levels.

title: School and career counselors and advisors
rationale: AI can assist with assessments, recordkeeping, résumé and application guidance, labor-market information, and identifying at-risk students from data, so the information-processing parts of the job are meaningfully exposed. But the core work still depends heavily on trust, empathy, sensitive judgment, and live interaction with students, families, and staff in school or counseling settings, which limits full automation.

title: Administrative services and facilities managers
rationale: This job mixes substantial computer-based planning, coordination, records management, policy review, and communication—areas where AI can boost productivity—with important onsite responsibilities like monitoring facilities, supervising maintenance staff, and handling building safety, repairs, and compliance. AI is likely to automate or streamline the administrative and analytical parts of the role, but the physical oversight, vendor coordination, and real-world judgment keep exposure below the highest tier.

rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: analyzing financial data, preparing reports and forecasts, monitoring compliance, and advising executives are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. title: Computer and information research scientists
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, coding, experimenting, analyzing results, and writing papers can largely be done on a computer, so AI is positioned to affect nearly every core task. Although frontier research, novel problem formulation, and interdisciplinary judgment still require substantial human expertise, AI is likely to sharply increase productivity and restructure how this work is done.

title: Fundraisers
rationale: Fundraising combines highly AI-exposed digital tasks—researching donors, drafting messages, analyzing campaign results, managing records, and running online outreach—with relationship-driven work that still benefits from human trust and persuasion. AI is likely to make fundraisers significantly more productive and reduce labor needed for routine prospecting and communications, but in-person donor cultivation and event-based fundraising keep exposure below the highest tier.

title: Actuaries
rationale: Actuarial work is overwhelmingly digital: analyzing data, building models, forecasting risk, pricing policies, and producing reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. title: Childcare workers
rationale: Childcare work is centered on physical presence, hands-on care, safety monitoring, and real-time interaction with children in unpredictable environments, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like recordkeeping, scheduling, activity planning, or parent communication, but it does not meaningfully replace the core work.
